speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape seven. Today is October 23, 1996. And we're interviewing Estelle Laughlin. You were just telling me your mother said [NON-ENGLISH], you were so beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, beautiful. And we were just, as I said, completely covered with scabies. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- during-- and, oh-- how we were clad. And we had wooden clogs and barefoot. And I remember when we used to walk on ice, the clogs would kind of slash and always cut. I always had these deep cuts, all of my whole leg. The inside my legs were covered with-- with scabs and oozing scabs and scabies and mange. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I used a-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the can, to urinate. I opened the door, I looked. And I poured the urine. But the Jewish couple, Mr. [? Aronovitz. ?] He-- he was OK. He was OK. He caught me. And he jumped on the bunk. And he was whipping me with-- with the whip, with the-- he was just whipping me. My mother flung her itself to protect me. She flung herself to cover me. And then my mother could be very dramatic, and but-- but her drama was sincere. And she said to him, you know, my blood will not rest in the grave for what you're doing to my child. And it will haunt you wherever you'll go for the rest of your life. And he stopped. Now, you would say, my goodness, what a horrendous person he was. But you have to also recognize that at the same time we had lots of rats and mice in camp. And the same-- the same commander, of the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] that [? sadist, ?] he gave orders that each kapo-- there were several kapos of each section-- had to deliver a certain amount of mice, a certain amount of rats. Because he wanted the camp to be free of rats. But the conditions were such that it was impossible. And he also, if there-- if he was-- if anything was out of order, he-- he would not only punish the kapos, but he would also kill. He would just take Jews out for sport. So in a way, what I did was endangering others. Everybody did it, but I was caught. I was caught so I was endangering others.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how-- how did it work finally to-- to help us? My sister had typhoid. And she was in this-- and I-- I don't understand why they let the people-- why they had a infirmary. Why? Because I don't know why they even bothered with having a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was like a little hospital, a little infirmary. And my sister had typhoid. And she was there in that hospital. But every time a German or that-- that commander would come, or another German, to shoot, he'd go. That Mr. Rabinowitz would go into the hospital and caution my sister. So feverishly, she would crawl out. He would help her hide. He would tell her to go to the latrine. So he saved her life that way. So that's how she was saved. And another incident. We worked in this big factory, everyone. We worked at night or in the morning. We would go to the factory. Our assignment was to scoop up the shells of-- I guess they must've been rifle shells. I don't know. They were about this length. And-- and they were shaped like a-- like a bullet, bullet-shaped, with a point. But they were hollow. And what we had to do is sort out the irregulars, the ones that did not have the right point. And this-- this was our jobs. And we had a-- a quota we had to do, complete, a-- a designated amount of that work. And-- there was-- there were Germans who were watching us. And there was a German woman who would, you know, if you fell asleep, a slap, slap. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had to somehow keep a little bit clean. I was the risk-taker in the family. My sister, was less of-- she was always the one who would say, oh, it's so undignifying, our existence. Let's surrender. Let's give up. And my mother and I were the ones that said-- you know, no. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the factory had-- the work that we did was only one small assignment. There were other-- other kinds of work, too. And there were big-- big-- big machines. It was a very noisy place. And one of the machines cleaned the bullets, the shells. And so there was a sudsy water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you had-- a-- at great risk, because it was sabotage-- you would walk with a can that was supposed to be your soup can with-- that had a little wire attached. And you'd walk. And you would walk by and dip and get that sudsy water that you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- we took that sudsy water, so we can wash ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we would do every now and then-- now I had scabies already on my head, on my scalp. But I was going to wash my hair. So my sister met me in the bathroom. And we were going to really quickly wash my hair. My sister and her friends, in her fear, forgot to add cold water. Because the water was scalding.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she scalds-- she pours that hot sudsy suds on my-- on my already-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my-- on my [NON-ENGLISH], we called it [NON-ENGLISH], on the scabies on my head. And she scalds it. And I had a bad infection. So there was a woman who was in charge of the sanitation, a Jewish woman. It was brought to her attention. And she was worried about my infection. And so she was looking for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was sort of one jump ahead. I knew that she knew. And I knew that she would look for me. So I got it into my head that she'll never find me and that if my hair goes, all of us will die. I There was just no way I would surrender.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of sleeping, I roamed on-- for-- for I don't know how many nights and how many days. I roamed and-- and on the lookout for her to come. Now, I was among the youngest in the camp. And everybody was moved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody-- when her name was Marisha, everyone went to her. She [? said ?] sick, no. She swore. She promised. But there was just no way that I-- that she was going to catch me. And she never did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's also one memorable thing. My sister's hair was shaved. And, yes. And-- and you know, my mother would have to, while she was in the hospital, my sister couldn't eat some of these things. So sometimes if it was not enough, my mother and I we would trade parts, not all, but parts of our rations to keep her alive. And she survived. And she's here and she's wonderful. While you were in the camps--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in any of the camps, did you have any news from the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall how long I was in this camp. But I know that I was in all the camps, from-- from Majdanek 1943 to 1945, in these three camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's finish off with the second camp and go on to the third camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All right. I don't know how long we were there. I would-- I remember that we certainly were there through at least one winter, if not-- if not two winters.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come--

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, excuse me. How did you come to leave this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day they just loaded us in the trains. And they took us to Czestochowa, which was also at Hasag, which was also a-- an ammunition factory. And we did essentially the same things. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it was pretty much the same kind of existence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, always horrible things would happen to-- constantly, of beatings, shootings, killings, dyings. For instance, one thing that comes to my mind is one woman who somehow, who came to Skarzysko, who was pregnant, a beautiful woman-- you see her pregnancy was not obvious right away. I don't know whether she came from Majdanek. The people in Skarzysko, the-- the Jewish people who were working in Skarzysko, came from various different parts of-- of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't-- I think, I am almost sure that she did come-- go through the selections and she did come from Majdanek and she was from Warsaw. But as she was there, her pregnancy advanced. And-- and she had the baby in the latrine. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just dropped. I mean, these-- these awful things were daily life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, you know, these visits from the Germans, particularly this guy who was the commander of the camp, these constant beatings. I remember once we were running, he made us run in a circle on the ice and whipped us and-- and insulted, screamed insults at us. And-- well, and then we went to Czestochowa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Czestochowa, essentially, we did the same thing. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hunger, tremendous hunger. To this day, if we go to a restaurant I'll always, always remark, mmm, the bread is so wonderful. And the potatoes are so wonderful. I don't think-- I cannot tell you, even to this day, the-- the taste of a crumb of bread dissolving in my mouth surpasses the most-- most exquisite food, and potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- I guess we can-- this is where we were liberated. And maybe I should proceed. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shortly before we were liberated-- but we had absolutely no-- no knowledge what was happening in the world and whether the Germans were winning or whether the Germans were losing. Of course, we always calculated, and speculated that, well, if they move us from this camp to that camp, what does it mean? Does it mean they're moving us closer to the Allies, further from the Allies? Does it mean that they are getting a licking? Is there hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, our biggest, biggest dream was to be-- the most-- most-- most welcome wish, would be to be bombed by-- by-- by-- by the Allies. That was the biggest wish. We used to really dream about that and talk about that. Every time we heard an airplane, oh, let them just send a few bombs. Let them just-- let them just, you know, bomb it. We had no knowledge what-- what was going on outside. At one point, I think that the Germans told us that they are going to save us, from-- which, you know, it just sounded so crazy. And we didn't know what to make of it. Now, my mother at that time was gray. And being gray and old meant that in a selection, she would be one of the early first candidates.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a little blue dye, cloth dye, that you dye cloth with. And we were going-- I don't know how we got it. But we were going to die her hair to save her. And there was-- I remember, we needed warm water for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that there was some kind of exhaust. And we were going-- we had it all figured out. That we going to-- We'll. Collect that steam and, you know, and the water, whatever water we get out of it. And we'll dye my mother's hair. Well, they told us that they are going to put us on the trains and save us from-- from the Allies. And we wanted to say, please, don't be so good. Don't save us. And we dyed my mother's hair. And she looked very, very funny. Because her hair was sort of very-- the color of my wall right there, very, very vibrant, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] almost an aqua, a very, very light blue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Between navy blue and-- like-- she looked-- it was strange. But anyway, her hair wasn't gray. And what was happening was that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- then one day-- they did not load us on the trains. And then maybe a day or so later, we did not go to work. So we were beginning to get a little bit suspicious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we began to pray for-- for Allied planes and Allied bombs. And we began to hear detonations in the distance. But we did not-- we did not stir from our-- our barracks. Because we always felt that they are luring us, you know. Who knows? s we knew that there was-- we thought that-- I don't know if there were any guards or not. But we thought that there were guards there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- we-- we met-- in Skarzysko, we met two of my cousins who were from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't think-- from a different town. That-- we did not really know them. We had-- we-- we knew them through letters. But we never saw them. My mother knew-- knew them. We knew of them. And my parents communicated-- but these two brothers. Now, they were all right in terms, they were able to smuggle in with them some-- some of their jewelry, some gold. And they-- they-- they-- they-- did not-- they did not starve. But anyway, we joined them in their barracks. And we just sat there. We went into their barracks. And we kept on hearing more and more detonation from-- from the distance. At some point, one or several of the men would step-- step outside the barrack and came back, hey, you know, there's no reaction from the outside. So they dared to go a little bit further. Then they came back and reported. And slowly, they reached the gate at night and there was-- and there was-- the gates where unguarded. But nobody-- we did not-- maybe some people dared out a little distance, I don't know. But it was a very gradual slow kind of testing. In the meantime, at night, the-- we-- the bombardment came closer and closer. Now, all I remember of that bombardment, the-- the-- it was very unthreatening to us. Because it was like Messiah was coming. And even if we are swallowed in-- in-- in the flames of Messiah, it was still all right. And-- but it was just so incredible after all of these years that it was just too much to really comprehend. And then in the morning, the-- the young men went out, tested to gates, went beyond the gates, came back in. But we still did not see the Allies. Well, just a short-- shortly after they came back, I said, oh, we, you know, saw Russian tanks. We were freed by the Russians. We saw the Russian tanks. Now, we are freed. My mother used to say, you know, our suffering makes us special. You'll see, when-- if-- if we survive, people will feel honored to touch us because-- because-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because of our suffering and-- and-- and our perseverance. Well, it was January. I think it was the 15th of January. I don't recall, but it was right in the middle of January. And the gates are flung open and, OK, you're free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my cousins took off. And they told us that they are going to go back to their house and that we should follow. We have-- we don't have a penny. We don't have a crumb of bread. We don't have anything. Where do we go? Well, we knew that we should move. We knew that we should move, because what if the Russian advance is pushed back? What if the Germans come back? In the meantime, we have to find shelter for the night. Where? So we march in our wooden shoes, barefoot. And-- a group of those-- those least able to take care of them, we kind of clung together. And we go into the city, into the town. And there they see this awful looking group of-- of people that looked like skeletons with these bulging eyes and some shaved and-- and skinny. And, oh, what is that? Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were so repulsive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so we were taunted on the way with-- you know, we were taunted. And-- and-- and-- you would think that the Polish people suffered a great, great deal during the war, that there would be some-- some identification and some-- some compassion, but very little. I certainly did not encounter any, neither did my sister or my mother, or the people in my group. And we found, finally, a place. Just-- just-- there was also a curfew. So we knew that we had to find shelter before-- before dark fell. And you know that in the winter, dusk, dark is-- is much earlier. We found a building, a school. And-- and we stayed in-- in that school. We lived in a closet. In a-- we used the shelf. It was-- it was a scholar-- a closet for school supplies. And we used-- we used this. And then from there we still felt we want to move, either find other relatives or go and find these two cousins. We wanted to-- to latch onto something. We were so helpless. And-- we knew that we had relatives who lived before the war in a town that is called Kielce that was not too awful far from Czestochowa. And it-- and it was on the way to where my cousins lived. And so we were moved-- we-- we-- whether we went on a train-- I think we went-- we-- we got on a train. And what we ate was, I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the only people who really helped us was one Jew would help another one. And if we would know that there was a field where there were potatoes, we would dug-- dig potatoes if we could find it. But it was-- it was-- it was a tremendous starvation. We begged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- and we made our way to Kielce. And in Kielce, we never found the cousins. They did not survive. And I remember when we approached, when we entered in Kielce, dusk was falling. And we were anxious to find shelter. And we were chased by-- by taunting Poles who were throwing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rocks and things at us. And a door opened. And an old woman who looked like a witch came out, was wearing this big skirt, and-- and a black babushka, toothless. She opened the door. And she said to all of us, come in, come in. And she gave us shelter, which was a very, very daring thing to do. Because, you know, that her neighbors will take it out on her. She was very, very poor. She just had this one room and the stove. She had a little borscht that she shared with us. And-- and we had shelter and warmth. And it was-- it's one person that I wish that I had been able or made an effort to find her, because she was very special.

speaker: interviewer
text: What steps did you take to rebuild your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a very, very long process. Eventually, we did find our way to our cousins. And in Kielce, the shelter that we found was-- there were-- there was one Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few Jewish men, Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a tailor. And in his-- he had a room in the building. And he led-- he invited Jewish people who were roaming. And so we were in his-- in his-- we had just a little plot, you know, large enough for us to stretch out, which was-- which was really a lot. And at one point-- should I pause now? Might as well. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
